In 2023, US$477 million, 1.4 percent of GDP, was spent on the Philippine military.[[360]](#cite_note-368)[[361]](#cite_note-369) Most of the country's defense spending is on the Philippine Army, which leads operations against internal threats such as [communist](/wiki/Communist_armed_conflicts_in_the_Philippines "Communist armed conflicts in the Philippines") and [Muslim](/wiki/Moro_conflict "Moro conflict") separatist insurgencies; its preoccupation with internal security contributed to the decline of [Philippine naval capability](/wiki/List_of_equipment_of_the_Philippine_Navy "List of equipment of the Philippine Navy") which began during the 1970s.[[362]](#cite_note-370) [A military modernization program](/wiki/AFP_Modernization_Act "AFP Modernization Act") began in 1995[[363]](#cite_note-371) and expanded in 2012 to build a more capable defense system.[[364]](#cite_note-372)

The Philippines has long struggled against [local insurgencies](/wiki/Civil_conflict_in_the_Philippines "Civil conflict in the Philippines"), [separatism](/wiki/Separatism_in_the_Philippines "Separatism in the Philippines"), and [terrorism](/wiki/Terrorism_in_the_Philippines "Terrorism in the Philippines").[[365]](#cite_note-373)[[366]](#cite_note-Croissant-2017-374)[[367]](#cite_note-375) [Bangsamoro](/wiki/Bangsamoro "Bangsamoro")'s largest separatist organizations, the [Moro National Liberation Front](/wiki/Moro_National_Liberation_Front "Moro National Liberation Front") and the [Moro Islamic Liberation Front](/wiki/Moro_Islamic_Liberation_Front "Moro Islamic Liberation Front"), signed final peace agreements with the government in [1996](/wiki/1996_Final_Peace_Agreement "1996 Final Peace Agreement") and [2014](/wiki/Comprehensive_Agreement_on_the_Bangsamoro "Comprehensive Agreement on the Bangsamoro") respectively.[[368]](#cite_note-376)[[369]](#cite_note-377) Other, more-militant groups such as [Abu Sayyaf](/wiki/Abu_Sayyaf "Abu Sayyaf") and [Bangsamoro Islamic Freedom Fighters](/wiki/Bangsamoro_Islamic_Freedom_Fighters "Bangsamoro Islamic Freedom Fighters")[[370]](#cite_note-CTCSentinel-BIFF-378) have kidnapped foreigners for ransom, particularly in the Sulu Archipelago[[371]](#cite_note-379)[[372]](#cite_note-380) and [Maguindanao](/wiki/Maguindanao "Maguindanao"),[[370]](#cite_note-CTCSentinel-BIFF-378) but their presence has been reduced.[[373]](#cite_note-381)[[374]](#cite_note-382)

The [Communist Party of the Philippines](/wiki/Communist_Party_of_the_Philippines "Communist Party of the Philippines") (CPP) and its military wing, the [New People's Army](/wiki/New_People%27s_Army "New People's Army") (NPA), have been waging [guerrilla warfare](/wiki/New_People%27s_Army_rebellion "New People's Army rebellion") against the government since the 1970s and have [engaged in ambushes, bombings, and assassinations](/wiki/Timeline_of_the_communist_rebellion_in_the_Philippines "Timeline of the communist rebellion in the Philippines") of government officials and security forces;[[375]](#cite_note-CIAWorldFactBook-2022-2023-383) although shrinking militarily and politically after the return of democracy in 1986,[[366]](#cite_note-Croissant-2017-374)[[376]](#cite_note-384) the CPP-NPA, through the [National Democratic Front of the Philippines](/wiki/National_Democratic_Front_of_the_Philippines "National Democratic Front of the Philippines"), continues to gather public support in urban areas by setting up [communist fronts](/wiki/Communist_front "Communist front"), infiltrating sectoral organizations, and rallying public discontent and increased militancy against the government.[[377]](#cite_note-385) The Philippines ranked 104th out of 163 countries in the 2024 [Global Peace Index](/wiki/Global_Peace_Index "Global Peace Index").[[378]](#cite_note-386)

### Administrative divisions

Main article: [Administrative divisions of the Philippines](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_the_Philippines "Administrative divisions of the Philippines")

[![Color-coded political map of the Philippines](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1b/Ph_regions_and_provinces.svg/330px-Ph_regions_and_provinces.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ph_regions_and_provinces.svg)

The Philippines' regions and provinces

The Philippines is divided into 18 [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_the_Philippines "Regions of the Philippines"), 82 [provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_the_Philippines "Provinces of the Philippines"), 149 [cities](/wiki/Cities_of_the_Philippines "Cities of the Philippines"), 1,493 [municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_of_the_Philippines "Municipalities of the Philippines"), and 42,011 [barangays](/wiki/Barangay "Barangay").[[379]](#cite_note-387) Regions other than [Bangsamoro](/wiki/Bangsamoro "Bangsamoro") are divided for administrative convenience.[[380]](#cite_note-388) [Calabarzon](/wiki/Calabarzon "Calabarzon") was the region with the greatest population as of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit). The [National Capital Region](/wiki/Metro_Manila "Metro Manila") (NCR) was the most densely populated.[[381]](#cite_note-PSA-2020-Census-Highlights-389)

The Philippines is a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"), with the exception of the [Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao](/wiki/Bangsamoro_Autonomous_Region_in_Muslim_Mindanao "Bangsamoro Autonomous Region in Muslim Mindanao") (BARMM).[[382]](#cite_note-390) There have been [steps towards decentralization](/wiki/Federalism_in_the_Philippines "Federalism in the Philippines").[[383]](#cite_note-391)[[384]](#cite_note-392) A 1991 law [devolved](/wiki/Devolution "Devolution") some powers to [local governments](/wiki/Local_government_in_the_Philippines "Local government in the Philippines").[[385]](#cite_note-393)

## Economy

Main article: [Economy of the Philippines](/wiki/Economy_of_the_Philippines "Economy of the Philippines")

The Philippine economy is the world's [34th largest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal) "List of countries by GDP (nominal)"), with an estimated 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit) nominal [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") of US$507.7 billion.[[386]](#cite_note-IMFWEO.PH-394) As a [newly industrialized country](/wiki/Newly_industrialized_country "Newly industrialized country"),[[387]](#cite_note-FederalRegister-Vol78-395)[[388]](#cite_note-396) the Philippine economy has been transitioning from an agricultural base to one with more emphasis on services and manufacturing.[[387]](#cite_note-FederalRegister-Vol78-395)[[389]](#cite_note-397) The country's labor force is about 50.89 million[[390]](#cite_note-ph-employmentrate-398) with an [unemployment rate](/wiki/List_of_sovereign_states_by_unemployment_rate "List of sovereign states by unemployment rate") of 5.8 percent as of January 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit).[[390]](#cite_note-ph-employmentrate-398) Gross international reserves totalled US$103.406 billion as of January 2024[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit).[[391]](#cite_note-399) [Debt-to-GDP ratio](/wiki/Debt-to-GDP_ratio "Debt-to-GDP ratio") decreased to 60.2 percent at the end of 2023 from a 17-year high 63.7 percent at the end of the third quarter of that year, and indicated resiliency during the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_the_Philippines "COVID-19 pandemic in the Philippines").[[392]](#cite_note-400) The country's unit of [currency](/wiki/Currency "Currency") is the [Philippine peso](/wiki/Philippine_peso "Philippine peso") (₱[[393]](#cite_note-401) or PHP[[394]](#cite_note-402)).[[395]](#cite_note-403)

The Philippines is a net importer,[[319]](#cite_note-ILOOrg-2019-327): 55–56, 61–65, 77, 83, 111[[396]](#cite_note-404) and a [debtor nation](/wiki/Net_international_investment_position "Net international investment position").[[397]](#cite_note-405) As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit), the country's main export markets were China, the United States, Japan, Hong Kong, and Singapore;[[398]](#cite_note-OECWorld-PH-406) primary exports included [integrated circuits](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit"), office machinery and parts, electrical transformers, insulated wiring, and [semiconductors](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor").[[398]](#cite_note-OECWorld-PH-406) Its primary import markets that year were China, Japan, South Korea, the United States, and Indonesia.[[398]](#cite_note-OECWorld-PH-406) Major export crops include [coconuts](/wiki/Coconut_production_in_the_Philippines "Coconut production in the Philippines"), [bananas](/wiki/Banana_industry#Production_and_export "Banana industry"), and [pineapples](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_pineapple_production "List of countries by pineapple production"); it is the world's largest producer of [abaca](/wiki/Abaca "Abaca"),[[210]](#cite_note-Boquet-2017-218): [226–242](https://books.google.com/books?id=90C4DgAAQBAJ&pg=PA226) and was the world's second biggest exporter of [nickel ore](/wiki/Nickel "Nickel") in 2022,[[399]](#cite_note-407) as well as the biggest exporter of gold-clad metals and the biggest importer of [copra](/wiki/Copra "Copra") in 2020.[[398]](#cite_note-OECWorld-PH-406)

[![Modern, landscaped office building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Head_Quarters_of_the_International_Rice_Research_Institute_in_Los_Ba%C3%B1os_-_panoramio.jpg/250px-Head_Quarters_of_the_International_Rice_Research_Institute_in_Los_Ba%C3%B1os_-_panoramio.jpg)](/wiki/File:Head_Quarters_of_the_International_Rice_Research_Institute_in_Los_Ba%C3%B1os_-_panoramio.jpg)

Headquarters of the [International Rice Research Institute](/wiki/International_Rice_Research_Institute "International Rice Research Institute") in [Los Baños, Laguna](/wiki/Los_Ba%C3%B1os,_Laguna "Los Baños, Laguna")

With an average annual growth rate of six to seven percent since around 2010, the Philippines has emerged as one of the world's fastest-growing economies,[[400]](#cite_note-408) driven primarily by its increasing reliance on the service sector.[[401]](#cite_note-OECD-SMEPolicyIndex2018-409) Regional development is uneven, however, with Manila (in particular) gaining most of the new economic growth.[[402]](#cite_note-410)[[403]](#cite_note-411) [Remittances](/wiki/Remittance "Remittance") from [overseas Filipinos](/wiki/Overseas_Filipinos "Overseas Filipinos"), mainly [seamen](/wiki/Sailor "Sailor")[[404]](#cite_note-412) and [nurses](/wiki/Nurses "Nurses"),[[405]](#cite_note-413) which the country is the world's largest suppliers of, contribute significantly to the country's economy;[[406]](#cite_note-414)[[401]](#cite_note-OECD-SMEPolicyIndex2018-409) they reached a record US$37.20 billion in 2023, accounting for 8.5 percent of GDP.[[407]](#cite_note-415) The Philippines is the world's primary [business process outsourcing](/wiki/Business_process_outsourcing_in_the_Philippines "Business process outsourcing in the Philippines") (BPO) center.[[408]](#cite_note-416)[[409]](#cite_note-417) About 1.3 million Filipinos work in the BPO sector, primarily in [customer service](/wiki/Customer_service "Customer service").[[410]](#cite_note-418) The country is a pioneer in digital finance, as the first country to put its entire national budget under the [blockchain](/wiki/Blockchain "Blockchain") system.[[411]](#cite_note-419)

### Science and technology

Main article: [Science and technology in the Philippines](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_the_Philippines "Science and technology in the Philippines")

Further information: [Philippine space program](/wiki/Philippine_space_program "Philippine space program")

The Philippines has one of the largest agricultural-research systems in Asia, despite relatively low spending on agricultural research and development.[[412]](#cite_note-420)[[413]](#cite_note-421) The country has developed new varieties of crops, including [rice](/wiki/Rice_production_in_the_Philippines "Rice production in the Philippines"),[[414]](#cite_note-422)[[415]](#cite_note-423) coconuts,[[416]](#cite_note-424) and bananas.[[417]](#cite_note-425) Research organizations include the [Philippine Rice Research Institute](/wiki/Philippine_Rice_Research_Institute "Philippine Rice Research Institute")[[418]](#cite_note-426) and the [International Rice Research Institute](/wiki/International_Rice_Research_Institute "International Rice Research Institute").[[419]](#cite_note-427)

The [Philippine Space Agency](/wiki/Philippine_Space_Agency "Philippine Space Agency") maintains the [country's space program](/wiki/Philippine_space_program "Philippine space program"),[[420]](#cite_note-428)[[421]](#cite_note-429) and the country bought its first [satellite](/wiki/List_of_Philippine_satellites "List of Philippine satellites") in 1996.[[422]](#cite_note-430) [Diwata-1](/wiki/Diwata-1 "Diwata-1"), its first [micro-satellite](/wiki/Small_satellite#Microsatellites "Small satellite"), was launched on the United States' [Cygnus](/wiki/Cygnus_(spacecraft) "Cygnus (spacecraft)") spacecraft in 2016.[[423]](#cite_note-431)

The Philippines has a [high concentration](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_smartphone_penetration "List of countries by smartphone penetration") of [cellular-phone](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") users,[[424]](#cite_note-432) and a high level of [mobile commerce](/wiki/Mobile_commerce "Mobile commerce").[[425]](#cite_note-433) [Text messaging](/wiki/Text_messaging "Text messaging") is a popular form of communication, and the nation sent an average of one billion [SMS](/wiki/SMS "SMS") messages per day in 2007.[[426]](#cite_note-434) The [Philippine telecommunications industry](/wiki/Telecommunications_in_the_Philippines "Telecommunications in the Philippines") had been dominated by the [PLDT](/wiki/PLDT "PLDT")-[Globe Telecom](/wiki/Globe_Telecom "Globe Telecom") duopoly for more than two decades,[[427]](#cite_note-435) and the 2021 entry of [Dito Telecommunity](/wiki/Dito_Telecommunity "Dito Telecommunity") improved the country's telecommunications service.[[428]](#cite_note-436) The Philippines was ranked 50th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[429]](#cite_note-437)[[430]](#cite_note-438)

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in the Philippines](/wiki/Tourism_in_the_Philippines "Tourism in the Philippines")

[![People on an observation deck overlooking hills](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Chocolate_Hills_and_tourists.jpg/250px-Chocolate_Hills_and_tourists.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chocolate_Hills_and_tourists.jpg)

Tourists at [Chocolate Hills](/wiki/Chocolate_Hills "Chocolate Hills"), conical [karst](/wiki/Karst "Karst") hills in [Bohol](/wiki/Bohol "Bohol")

The Philippines is a popular retirement destination for foreigners because of its climate and low cost of living.[[431]](#cite_note-439) The country's main tourist attractions are [its numerous beaches](/wiki/List_of_beaches_in_the_Philippines "List of beaches in the Philippines");[[58]](#cite_note-Woods-2006-65): [109](https://books.google.com/books?id=eajOEAAAQBAJ&pg=109)[[432]](#cite_note-440) the Philippines is also a top destination for diving enthusiasts.[[433]](#cite_note-441)[[434]](#cite_note-442) [Tourist spots](/wiki/List_of_tourist_attractions_in_the_Philippines "List of tourist attractions in the Philippines") include [Boracay](/wiki/Boracay "Boracay"), called the best island in the world by *[Travel + Leisure](/wiki/Travel_%2B_Leisure "Travel + Leisure")* in 2012;[[435]](#cite_note-443) [Coron](/wiki/Coron,_Palawan "Coron, Palawan") and [El Nido](/wiki/El_Nido,_Palawan "El Nido, Palawan") in Palawan; [Cebu](/wiki/Cebu "Cebu"); [Siargao](/wiki/Siargao "Siargao"), and [Bohol](/wiki/Bohol "Bohol").[[436]](#cite_note-444)

Tourism contributed 5.2 percent to the Philippine GDP in 2021 (lower than 12.7 percent in 2019, before the COVID-19 pandemic),[[437]](#cite_note-445) and provided 5.7 million jobs in 2019.[[438]](#cite_note-446) The Philippines attracted 5.45 million international visitors in 2023, 30 percent lower than the 8.26 million record in pre-pandemic 2019; most tourists came from South Korea (26.4 percent), United States (16.5 percent), Japan (5.6 percent), Australia (4.89 percent), and China (4.84 percent).[[439]](#cite_note-447)

## Infrastructure

### Transportation

Main article: [Transportation in the Philippines](/wiki/Transportation_in_the_Philippines "Transportation in the Philippines")

[![Two white buses side by side, one larger than the other](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Traditional_and_modern_jeepneys_in_Diliman%2C_Quezon_City_on_March_16%2C_2023.jpg/250px-Traditional_and_modern_jeepneys_in_Diliman%2C_Quezon_City_on_March_16%2C_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traditional_and_modern_jeepneys_in_Diliman,_Quezon_City_on_March_16,_2023.jpg)

Traditional *(left)* and modern [jeepneys](/wiki/Jeepney "Jeepney") in [Quezon City](/wiki/Quezon_City "Quezon City"). Public utility vehicles older than 15 years are [gradually being phased out](/wiki/Public_Utility_Vehicle_Modernization_Program "Public Utility Vehicle Modernization Program") in favor of eco-friendly [Euro 4](/wiki/European_emission_standards "European emission standards")-compliant vehicles.[[440]](#cite_note-448)

Transportation in the Philippines is by road, air, rail and water. Roads are the dominant form of transport, carrying 98 percent of people and 58 percent of cargo.[[441]](#cite_note-ADBOrg-PH-Transport-449) In December 2018, there were 210,528 kilometers (130,816 mi) of roads in the country.[[442]](#cite_note-450) The backbone of land-based transportation in the country is the [Pan-Philippine Highway](/wiki/Pan-Philippine_Highway "Pan-Philippine Highway"), which connects the islands of Luzon, Samar, Leyte, and Mindanao.[[443]](#cite_note-451) Inter-island transport is by the 919-kilometer (571 mi) [Strong Republic Nautical Highway](/wiki/Strong_Republic_Nautical_Highway "Strong Republic Nautical Highway"), an integrated set of highways and [ferry](/wiki/Ferry "Ferry") routes linking 17 cities.[[444]](#cite_note-452)[[445]](#cite_note-453) [Jeepneys](/wiki/Jeepney "Jeepney") are a popular, iconic public utility vehicle;[[210]](#cite_note-Boquet-2017-218): [496–497](https://books.google.com/books?id=90C4DgAAQBAJ&pg=PA496) other public land transport includes [buses](/wiki/List_of_bus_companies_of_the_Philippines "List of bus companies of the Philippines"), [UV Express](/wiki/UV_Express "UV Express"), TNVS, Filcab, [taxis](/wiki/Taxis_of_the_Philippines "Taxis of the Philippines"), and [tricycles](/wiki/Motorized_tricycle_(Philippines) "Motorized tricycle (Philippines)").[[446]](#cite_note-454)[[447]](#cite_note-455) Traffic is a significant issue [in Manila](/wiki/Traffic_in_Metro_Manila "Traffic in Metro Manila") and on arterial roads to the capital.[[448]](#cite_note-456)[[449]](#cite_note-457)

Despite wider historical use,[[450]](#cite_note-458) [rail transportation in the Philippines](/wiki/Rail_transportation_in_the_Philippines "Rail transportation in the Philippines") is limited[[210]](#cite_note-Boquet-2017-218): [491](https://books.google.com/books?id=90C4DgAAQBAJ&pg=PA491) to [transporting passengers within Metro Manila](/wiki/Rail_transportation_in_the_Greater_Manila_Area "Rail transportation in the Greater Manila Area") and the provinces of [Laguna](/wiki/Laguna_(province) "Laguna (province)")[[451]](#cite_note-459) and [Quezon](/wiki/Quezon "Quezon"),[[452]](#cite_note-460) with a short track in the [Bicol Region](/wiki/Bicol_Region "Bicol Region").[[210]](#cite_note-Boquet-2017-218): [491](https://books.google.com/books?id=90C4DgAAQBAJ&pg=PA491) The country had a railway footprint of only 79 kilometers (49 mi) as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit), which it planned to expand to 244 kilometers (152 mi).[[453]](#cite_note-461) A revival of [freight rail](/wiki/Freight_rail "Freight rail") is planned to reduce road congestion.[[454]](#cite_note-462)[[455]](#cite_note-463)

The Philippines had [90 national government-owned airports](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_the_Philippines "List of airports in the Philippines") as of 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit), of which eight are [international](/wiki/International_airport "International airport").[[456]](#cite_note-PIDSGovPH-Francisco-Lim-464) [Ninoy Aquino International Airport](/wiki/Ninoy_Aquino_International_Airport "Ninoy Aquino International Airport"), formerly known as Manila International Airport, has the [greatest number of passengers](/wiki/List_of_the_busiest_airports_in_the_Philippines "List of the busiest airports in the Philippines").[[456]](#cite_note-PIDSGovPH-Francisco-Lim-464) The 2017 [air domestic market](/wiki/List_of_airlines_of_the_Philippines "List of airlines of the Philippines") was dominated by [Philippine Airlines](/wiki/Philippine_Airlines "Philippine Airlines"), the country's [flag carrier](/wiki/Flag_carrier "Flag carrier") and Asia's oldest commercial airline,[[457]](#cite_note-465)[[458]](#cite_note-466) and [Cebu Pacific](/wiki/Cebu_Pacific "Cebu Pacific") (the country's leading [low-cost carrier](/wiki/Low-cost_carrier "Low-cost carrier")).[[459]](#cite_note-467)[[460]](#cite_note-468)

A variety of boats are used throughout the Philippines;[[461]](#cite_note-469) most are [double-outrigger](/wiki/Outrigger_boat "Outrigger boat") vessels known as *banca*[[462]](#cite_note-Aguilar-2004-470) or *[bangka](/wiki/Bangka_(boat) "Bangka (boat)")*.[[463]](#cite_note-Funtecha-2000-471) Modern ships use plywood instead of logs, and motor engines instead of sails;[[462]](#cite_note-Aguilar-2004-470) they are used for fishing and inter-island travel.[[463]](#cite_note-Funtecha-2000-471) The Philippines has [over 1,800 seaports](/wiki/List_of_ports_in_the_Philippines "List of ports in the Philippines");[[464]](#cite_note-472) of these, the principal seaports of [Manila](/wiki/Port_of_Manila "Port of Manila") (the country's chief, and busiest, port),[[465]](#cite_note-473) [Batangas](/wiki/Batangas_International_Port "Batangas International Port"), [Subic Bay](/wiki/Port_of_Subic_Bay "Port of Subic Bay"), [Cebu](/wiki/Port_of_Cebu "Port of Cebu"), [Iloilo](/wiki/Port_of_Iloilo "Port of Iloilo"), [Davao](/wiki/Port_of_Davao "Port of Davao"), [Cagayan de Oro](/wiki/Port_of_Cagayan_de_Oro "Port of Cagayan de Oro"), [General Santos](/wiki/Port_of_General_Santos "Port of General Santos"), and [Zamboanga](/wiki/Port_of_Zamboanga "Port of Zamboanga") are part of the ASEAN Transport Network.[[466]](#cite_note-474)[[467]](#cite_note-475)

### Energy

Main article: [Energy in the Philippines](/wiki/Energy_in_the_Philippines "Energy in the Philippines")

[![A large dam, seen from above](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5c/Ambuklao_Dam_captured_by_Mitchell_Yumul.jpeg/250px-Ambuklao_Dam_captured_by_Mitchell_Yumul.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Ambuklao_Dam_captured_by_Mitchell_Yumul.jpeg)

The [Ambuklao Dam](/wiki/Ambuklao_Dam "Ambuklao Dam") on the [Agno River](/wiki/Agno_River "Agno River") in [Bokod, Benguet](/wiki/Bokod,_Benguet "Bokod, Benguet")

The Philippines had a total installed power capacity of 26,882 [MW](/wiki/Watt "Watt") in 2021; 43 percent was generated from [coal](/wiki/Coal_mining_in_the_Philippines "Coal mining in the Philippines"), 14 percent from [oil](/wiki/Oil "Oil"), 14 percent [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower"), 12 percent from [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), and seven percent from [geothermal](/wiki/Geothermal_power "Geothermal power") sources.[[468]](#cite_note-476) It is the world's third-biggest [geothermal-energy producer](/wiki/Geothermal_power_in_the_Philippines "Geothermal power in the Philippines"), behind the United States and Indonesia.[[469]](#cite_note-477) The country's largest dam is the 1.2-kilometer-long (0.75 mi) [San Roque Dam](/wiki/San_Roque_Dam_(Philippines) "San Roque Dam (Philippines)") on the [Agno River](/wiki/Agno_River "Agno River") in [Pangasinan](/wiki/Pangasinan "Pangasinan").[[470]](#cite_note-478) The [Malampaya gas field](/wiki/Malampaya_gas_field "Malampaya gas field"), discovered in the early 1990s off the coast of Palawan, reduced the Philippines' reliance on imported oil; it provides about 40 percent of Luzon's energy requirements, and 30 percent of the country's energy needs.[[210]](#cite_note-Boquet-2017-218): [347](https://books.google.com/books?id=90C4DgAAQBAJ&pg=PA347)[[471]](#cite_note-479)

The Philippines has three [electrical grids](/wiki/Electrical_grid "Electrical grid"), one each for Luzon, Visayas, and Mindanao.[[472]](#cite_note-Ma-2016-480) The [National Grid Corporation of the Philippines](/wiki/National_Grid_Corporation_of_the_Philippines "National Grid Corporation of the Philippines") manages [the country's power grid](/wiki/Electricity_sector_in_the_Philippines "Electricity sector in the Philippines") since 2009[[473]](#cite_note-481) and provides [overhead transmission lines](/wiki/Overhead_power_line "Overhead power line") across the country's islands. Electric distribution to consumers [is provided](/wiki/List_of_electric_distribution_utilities_in_the_Philippines "List of electric distribution utilities in the Philippines") by privately owned distribution utilities and government-owned [electric cooperatives](/wiki/Electric_cooperative "Electric cooperative").[[472]](#cite_note-Ma-2016-480) As of end-2021, the Philippines' household electrification level was about 95.41%.[[474]](#cite_note-482)

Plans to harness [nuclear energy](/wiki/Nuclear_power_in_the_Philippines "Nuclear power in the Philippines") began during the early 1970s during the [presidency of Ferdinand Marcos](/wiki/Presidency_of_Ferdinand_Marcos "Presidency of Ferdinand Marcos") in response to the [1973 oil crisis](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis").[[475]](#cite_note-WorldNuclearOrg-Philippines-483) The Philippines completed [Southeast Asia's first nuclear power plant](/wiki/Bataan_Nuclear_Power_Plant "Bataan Nuclear Power Plant") in [Bataan](/wiki/Bataan "Bataan") in 1984.[[476]](#cite_note-Pekkanen-2021-484) Political issues following Marcos' ouster and safety concerns after the 1986 [Chernobyl disaster](/wiki/Chernobyl_disaster "Chernobyl disaster") prevented the plant from being commissioned,[[477]](#cite_note-485)[[475]](#cite_note-WorldNuclearOrg-Philippines-483) and plans to operate it remain controversial.[[476]](#cite_note-Pekkanen-2021-484)[[478]](#cite_note-486)

### Water supply and sanitation

Main article: [Water supply and sanitation in the Philippines](/wiki/Water_supply_and_sanitation_in_the_Philippines "Water supply and sanitation in the Philippines")

[![A low, blue building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Banate_Water_District.jpg/250px-Banate_Water_District.jpg)](/wiki/File:Banate_Water_District.jpg)

A water-district office in [Banate, Iloilo](/wiki/Banate,_Iloilo "Banate, Iloilo")

Water supply and sanitation outside Metro Manila is provided by the government through local [water districts](/wiki/Water_district "Water district") in cities or towns.[[479]](#cite_note-487)[[480]](#cite_note-Kohsaka-2007-488)[[481]](#cite_note-489) Metro Manila is served by [Manila Water](/wiki/Manila_Water "Manila Water") and [Maynilad Water Services](/wiki/Maynilad_Water_Services "Maynilad Water Services"). Except for shallow wells for domestic use, groundwater users are required to obtain a permit from the [National Water Resources Board](/wiki/National_Water_Resources_Board "National Water Resources Board").[[480]](#cite_note-Kohsaka-2007-488) In 2022, the total water withdrawals increased to 91 billion cubic meters (3.2×10~~^~~12 cu ft) from 89 billion cubic meters (3.1×10~~^~~12 cu ft) in 2021 and the total expenditures on water were amounted to [₱](/wiki/Philippine_peso "Philippine peso")144.81 billion.[[482]](#cite_note-490)

Most sewage in the Philippines flows into septic tanks.[[480]](#cite_note-Kohsaka-2007-488) In 2015, the [Joint Monitoring Programme for Water Supply and Sanitation](/wiki/Joint_Monitoring_Programme_for_Water_Supply_and_Sanitation "Joint Monitoring Programme for Water Supply and Sanitation") noted that 74 percent of the Philippine population had access to [improved sanitation](/wiki/Improved_sanitation "Improved sanitation") and "good progress" had been made between 1990 and 2015.[[483]](#cite_note-491) 96 percent of Filipino households had an improved source of drinking water and 92 percent of households had sanitary toilet facilities as of 2016[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit); connections of toilet facilities to appropriate sewerage systems remain largely insufficient, however, especially in rural and urban poor communities.[[484]](#cite_note-DOH-2018-492): 46

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of the Philippines](/wiki/Demographics_of_the_Philippines "Demographics of the Philippines")

See also: [List of cities in the Philippines](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_the_Philippines "List of cities in the Philippines")

In July 2024, the Philippines had a population of 112,729,484.[[11]](#cite_note-PSA_POPCEN-16) More than 60 percent of the country's population live in the [coastal zone](/wiki/Coastal_zone "Coastal zone").[[485]](#cite_note-493) In 2020, 54 percent lived in [urban areas](/wiki/Urban_area "Urban area").[[486]](#cite_note-PSAGovPH-2020-Urban-494) [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila"), its capital, and [Quezon City](/wiki/Quezon_City "Quezon City"), the country's most populous city, are in [Metro Manila](/wiki/Metro_Manila "Metro Manila"). About 13.48 million people, 12 percent of the Philippines' population, live in Metro Manila,[[486]](#cite_note-PSAGovPH-2020-Urban-494) the [country's most populous metropolitan area](/wiki/List_of_metropolitan_areas_in_the_Philippines "List of metropolitan areas in the Philippines")[[487]](#cite_note-495) and the world's [fifth most populous](/wiki/List_of_largest_cities "List of largest cities").[[488]](#cite_note-496) Between 1948 and 2010, the population of the Philippines increased almost fivefold, from 19 million to 92 million.[[489]](#cite_note-497)

The [country's median age](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_median_age "List of countries by median age") is 25.3, and 63.9 percent of its population is between 15 and 64 years old.[[490]](#cite_note-498) The Philippines' average annual population growth rate is decreasing,[[491]](#cite_note-499) although government attempts to further reduce population growth [have been contentious](/wiki/Reproductive_Health_Bill_(Philippines) "Reproductive Health Bill (Philippines)").[[492]](#cite_note-500) The country [reduced its poverty rate](/wiki/Poverty_in_the_Philippines "Poverty in the Philippines") from 49.2 percent in 1985,[[493]](#cite_note-WorldBank-Poverty-2023-501) to 18.1 percent in 2021.[[494]](#cite_note-502) [The Philippines' income inequality](/wiki/Income_inequality_in_the_Philippines "Income inequality in the Philippines") began to decline in 2012.[[493]](#cite_note-WorldBank-Poverty-2023-501)
