### Ethnicity

Main article: [Ethnic groups in the Philippines](/wiki/Ethnic_groups_in_the_Philippines "Ethnic groups in the Philippines")

See also: [Filipinos](/wiki/Filipinos "Filipinos"), [Pinoy](/wiki/Pinoy "Pinoy"), and [Genetic and anthropology studies on Filipinos](/wiki/Genetic_and_anthropology_studies_on_Filipinos "Genetic and anthropology studies on Filipinos")

[![Another color-coded map](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Peoples_of_the_Philippines_en.svg/250px-Peoples_of_the_Philippines_en.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Peoples_of_the_Philippines_en.svg)

Dominant ethnic groups by province

The Philippines has substantial ethnic diversity, due to foreign influence and the archipelago's division by water and topography.[[288]](#cite_note-Banlaoi-2009-296) In the 2020 census, the Philippines' largest ethnic groups were [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_people "Tagalog people") (26.0 percent), [Visayans](/wiki/Visayans "Visayans") [excluding the [Cebuano](/wiki/Cebuano_people "Cebuano people"), [Hiligaynon](/wiki/Hiligaynon_people "Hiligaynon people"), and [Waray](/wiki/Waray_people "Waray people")] (14.3 percent), [Ilocano](/wiki/Ilocano_people "Ilocano people") and Cebuano (both eight percent), Hiligaynon (7.9 percent), [Bikol](/wiki/Bicolano_people "Bicolano people") (6.5 percent), and Waray (3.8 percent).[[6]](#cite_note-PSAGovPH-Ethnicity-2020Census-9) The [country's indigenous peoples](/wiki/Indigenous_peoples_of_the_Philippines "Indigenous peoples of the Philippines") consisted of 110 enthnolinguistic groups,[[495]](#cite_note-503) with a combined population of 15.56 million, in 2020.[[6]](#cite_note-PSAGovPH-Ethnicity-2020Census-9) They include the [Igorot](/wiki/Igorot_people "Igorot people"), [Lumad](/wiki/Lumad "Lumad"), [Mangyan](/wiki/Mangyan "Mangyan"), and the [indigenous peoples of Palawan](/wiki/Peoples_of_Palawan "Peoples of Palawan").[[496]](#cite_note-504)

[Negritos](/wiki/Negrito "Negrito") are thought to be among the islands' earliest inhabitants.[[82]](#cite_note-Dolan-1991-89): [35](https://web.archive.org/web/20060927160916/http://countrystudies.us/philippines/35.htm) These minority aboriginal settlers are an [Australoid](/wiki/Australo-Melanesian "Australo-Melanesian") group, a remnant of the [first human migration from Africa to Australia](/wiki/Southern_Dispersal "Southern Dispersal") who were probably displaced by later waves of migration.[[497]](#cite_note-505) Some Philippine Negritos have a [Denisovan](/wiki/Denisovan "Denisovan") admixture in their [genome](/wiki/Genome "Genome").[[498]](#cite_note-506)[[499]](#cite_note-507) Ethnic Filipinos generally belong to several Southeast Asian ethnic groups, classified linguistically as [Austronesians](/wiki/Austronesian_peoples "Austronesian peoples") speaking [Malayo-Polynesian languages](/wiki/Malayo-Polynesian_languages "Malayo-Polynesian languages").[[500]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-PH-508)

The Austronesian population's origin is uncertain, but relatives of [Taiwanese aborigines](/wiki/Taiwanese_indigenous_peoples "Taiwanese indigenous peoples") probably brought their language and mixed with the region's existing population.[[501]](#cite_note-509)[[502]](#cite_note-510) The Lumad and [Sama-Bajau](/wiki/Sama-Bajau "Sama-Bajau") ethnic groups have an ancestral affinity with the [Austroasiatic](/wiki/Austroasiatic_languages "Austroasiatic languages") and [Mlabri-speaking](/wiki/Mlabri_language "Mlabri language") [Htin](/wiki/Lua_people "Lua people") peoples of mainland Southeast Asia. Westward expansion of [Papuan ancestry](/wiki/Papuan_languages "Papuan languages") from [Papua New Guinea](/wiki/Papua_New_Guinea "Papua New Guinea") to eastern Indonesia and [Mindanao](/wiki/Mindanao "Mindanao") has been detected in the [Blaan people](/wiki/Blaan_people "Blaan people") and the [Sangir language](/wiki/Sangir_language "Sangir language"), while ancient immigration added some [Indian](/wiki/India "India") ancestry to the precolonial [Indianized kingdoms](/wiki/History_of_the_Philippines_(900%E2%80%931565) "History of the Philippines (900–1565)") of the islands.[[503]](#cite_note-511)

Immigrants arrived in the Philippines from elsewhere in the Spanish Empire, especially [from the Spanish Americas](/wiki/Latin_American_Asian "Latin American Asian").[[504]](#cite_note-512)[[505]](#cite_note-Mehl-2016-513): [Chpt. 6](https://web.archive.org/web/20180614082235/https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/forced-migration-in-the-spanish-pacific-world/unruly-mexicans-in-manila/EF2599210A0715A5A91B23BB9D84B96C)[[506]](#cite_note-Intercolonial-514) A 2016 [National Geographic](/wiki/Geno_2.0_Next_Generation "Geno 2.0 Next Generation") project [concluded](/wiki/Genetic_studies_on_Filipinos "Genetic studies on Filipinos") that people living in the Philippine archipelago carried [genetic markers](/wiki/Genetic_marker "Genetic marker") in the following percentages: 53 percent [Southeast Asia](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia") and [Oceania](/wiki/Oceania "Oceania"), 36 percent [East Asia](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), 5 percent [Southern Europe](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe"), 3 percent [Southern Asia](/wiki/South_Asia "South Asia"), and 2 percent Native American (from [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America")).[[505]](#cite_note-Mehl-2016-513): [Chpt. 6](https://web.archive.org/web/20180614082235/https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/forced-migration-in-the-spanish-pacific-world/unruly-mexicans-in-manila/EF2599210A0715A5A91B23BB9D84B96C)[[507]](#cite_note-515)

Descendants of mixed-race couples are known as [Mestizos](/wiki/Filipino_Mestizos "Filipino Mestizos") or *tisoy*,[[508]](#cite_note-516) which during the [Spanish colonial times](/wiki/History_of_the_Philippines_(1565%E2%80%931898) "History of the Philippines (1565–1898)"), were mostly composed of [Chinese mestizos](/wiki/Sangley "Sangley") (*Mestizos de Sangley*), [Spanish mestizos](/wiki/Spanish_Filipino "Spanish Filipino") (*Mestizos de Español*) and the mix thereof (*[tornatrás](/wiki/Torna_atr%C3%A1s "Torna atrás")*).[[509]](#cite_note-517)[[510]](#cite_note-518)[[511]](#cite_note-519) The modern [Chinese Filipinos](/wiki/Chinese_Filipino "Chinese Filipino") are well-integrated into Filipino society.[[288]](#cite_note-Banlaoi-2009-296)[[512]](#cite_note-520) Primarily the descendants of immigrants from [Fujian](/wiki/Fujian "Fujian"),[[513]](#cite_note-521) the pure ethnic Chinese Filipinos during the [American colonial era](/wiki/History_of_the_Philippines_(1898%E2%80%931946) "History of the Philippines (1898–1946)") (early 1900s) purportedly numbered about 1.35 million; while an estimated 22.8 million (around 20 percent) of Filipinos have half or partial Chinese ancestry from precolonial, colonial, and 20th century [Chinese migrants](/wiki/Overseas_Chinese "Overseas Chinese").[[514]](#cite_note-522)[[515]](#cite_note-senate.gov.ph-523) During the Hispanic era (late 1700s), the tribute-census showed that [mixed Spanish Filipinos](/wiki/Spanish_Filipino "Spanish Filipino") made up a moderate ratio (around 5 percent) of all citizens.[[516]](#cite_note-524): 539[[517]](#cite_note-525): 31, 54, 113 Meanwhile, [a smaller proportion (2.33 percent) of the population were Mexican Filipinos](/wiki/Mexican_settlement_in_the_Philippines "Mexican settlement in the Philippines").[[506]](#cite_note-Intercolonial-514): 100 Almost 300,000 [American citizens](/wiki/Americans_in_the_Philippines "Americans in the Philippines") live in the country as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit),[[518]](#cite_note-526) this increased to 750,000 by year 2025,[[519]](#cite_note-ManilaBulletin-527) (forming 0.75% of the demographics)[[519]](#cite_note-ManilaBulletin-527) and up to 250,000 [Amerasians](/wiki/Amerasian "Amerasian"),[[520]](#cite_note-AmerasianResearch-528) (0.25% of the country), are in the cities of [Clark](/wiki/Clark_Global_City "Clark Global City"), [Angeles](/wiki/Angeles_City "Angeles City"), Manila, and [Olongapo](/wiki/Olongapo "Olongapo")[[520]](#cite_note-AmerasianResearch-528)[[521]](#cite_note-529) (When combined, [American](/wiki/Americans "Americans")[[519]](#cite_note-ManilaBulletin-527) and [Amerasian](/wiki/Amerasian "Amerasian") descent,[[520]](#cite_note-AmerasianResearch-528) form 1% of the population).[[519]](#cite_note-ManilaBulletin-527)[[520]](#cite_note-AmerasianResearch-528) Other significant non-indigenous minorities include [Indians](/wiki/Indian_Filipino "Indian Filipino")[[522]](#cite_note-530) and [Arabs](/wiki/Arabs_in_the_Philippines "Arabs in the Philippines").[[523]](#cite_note-531) [Japanese Filipinos](/wiki/Japanese_in_the_Philippines "Japanese in the Philippines") include escaped Christians ([Kirishitan](/wiki/Kirishitan "Kirishitan")) who [fled persecutions by Shogun Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Martyrs_of_Japan "Martyrs of Japan").[[524]](#cite_note-532)

### Languages

Main article: [Languages of the Philippines](/wiki/Languages_of_the_Philippines "Languages of the Philippines")

*[Ethnologue](/wiki/Ethnologue "Ethnologue")* lists 186 languages for the Philippines, 182 of which are [living languages](/wiki/Living_language "Living language"). The other four no longer have any known speakers. Most native languages are part of the [Philippine branch](/wiki/Philippine_languages "Philippine languages") of the [Malayo-Polynesian languages](/wiki/Malayo-Polynesian_languages "Malayo-Polynesian languages"), which is a branch of the [Austronesian language family](/wiki/Austronesian_languages "Austronesian languages").[[500]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-PH-508) Spanish-based [creole](/wiki/Creole_language "Creole language") varieties, collectively known as [Chavacano](/wiki/Chavacano "Chavacano"), are also spoken.[[525]](#cite_note-533) Many [Philippine Negrito languages](/wiki/Philippine_Negrito_languages#Unique_vocabulary "Philippine Negrito languages") have unique vocabularies which survived Austronesian acculturation.[[526]](#cite_note-534)

[Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_language "Filipino language") and [English](/wiki/Philippine_English "Philippine English") are the country's official languages.[[5]](#cite_note-GovPH-OfficialLanguage-7) Filipino, a [standardized version](/wiki/Standard_language "Standard language") of [Tagalog](/wiki/Tagalog_language "Tagalog language"), is spoken primarily in Metro Manila.[[527]](#cite_note-535) Filipino and English are used in government, education, print, broadcast media, and business, often with a third local language.[[528]](#cite_note-Brown-Ganguly-2003-536) [Code-switching](/wiki/Code-switching "Code-switching") between English and other local languages, notably [Tagalog](/wiki/Taglish "Taglish"), is common.[[529]](#cite_note-537) The Philippine constitution provides for [Spanish](/wiki/Philippine_Spanish "Philippine Spanish") and Arabic on a voluntary, optional basis.[[5]](#cite_note-GovPH-OfficialLanguage-7) Spanish, a widely used [lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca") during the late nineteenth century, [has declined greatly in use](/wiki/Spanish_language_in_the_Philippines "Spanish language in the Philippines"),[[530]](#cite_note-538)[[531]](#cite_note-539) although Spanish [loanwords](/wiki/Loanword "Loanword") are still present in Philippine languages.[[532]](#cite_note-540)[[533]](#cite_note-:2-541)[[534]](#cite_note-542) Arabic is primarily taught in [Mindanao](/wiki/Mindanao "Mindanao") Islamic schools.[[535]](#cite_note-543)

In 2020, the top languages generally spoken at home were Tagalog, [Binisaya](/wiki/Binisaya "Binisaya"), [Hiligaynon](/wiki/Hiligaynon_language "Hiligaynon language"), [Ilocano](/wiki/Ilocano_language "Ilocano language"), [Cebuano](/wiki/Cebuano_language "Cebuano language"), and [Bikol](/wiki/Bikol_languages "Bikol languages").[[536]](#cite_note-544) Nineteen [regional languages](/wiki/List_of_regional_languages_of_the_Philippines "List of regional languages of the Philippines") are auxiliary official languages as media of instruction:[[4]](#cite_note-GMA-DepEd-7-Languages-6)

* [Aklanon](/wiki/Aklanon_language "Aklanon language")
* Bikol
* Cebuano
* [Chavacano](/wiki/Chavacano "Chavacano")
* Hiligaynon
* [Ibanag](/wiki/Ibanag_language "Ibanag language")
* Ilocano
* [Ivatan](/wiki/Ivatan_language "Ivatan language")
* [Kapampangan](/wiki/Kapampangan_language "Kapampangan language")
* [Kinaray-a](/wiki/Karay-a_language "Karay-a language")
* [Maguindanao](/wiki/Maguindanao_language "Maguindanao language")
* [Maranao](/wiki/Maranao_language "Maranao language")
* [Pangasinan](/wiki/Pangasinan_language "Pangasinan language")
* [Sambal](/wiki/Sambal_language "Sambal language")
* [Surigaonon](/wiki/Surigaonon_language "Surigaonon language")
* Tagalog
* [Tausug](/wiki/Tausug_language "Tausug language")
* [Waray](/wiki/Waray_language "Waray language")
* [Yakan](/wiki/Yakan_language "Yakan language")

Other indigenous languages, including [Cuyonon](/wiki/Cuyonon_language "Cuyonon language"), [Ifugao](/wiki/Ifugao_language "Ifugao language"), [Itbayat](/wiki/Itbayat_language "Itbayat language"), [Kalinga](/wiki/Kalinga_language "Kalinga language"), [Kamayo](/wiki/Kamayo_language "Kamayo language"), [Kankanaey](/wiki/Kankanaey_language "Kankanaey language"), [Masbateño](/wiki/Masbate%C3%B1o_language "Masbateño language"), [Romblomanon](/wiki/Romblomanon_language "Romblomanon language"), [Manobo](/wiki/Manobo_languages "Manobo languages"), and several [Visayan languages](/wiki/Visayan_languages "Visayan languages"), are used in their respective provinces.[[500]](#cite_note-Ethnologue-PH-508) [Filipino Sign Language](/wiki/Filipino_Sign_Language "Filipino Sign Language") is the national sign language, and the language of [deaf education](/wiki/Deafness_in_the_Philippines "Deafness in the Philippines").[[537]](#cite_note-545)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in the Philippines](/wiki/Religion_in_the_Philippines "Religion in the Philippines")

[![Large crowd outside a colorfully-decorated church](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Sto._Nino_Basilica_de_Cebu_from_the_Pligrim%27s_Center.jpg/250px-Sto._Nino_Basilica_de_Cebu_from_the_Pligrim%27s_Center.jpg)](/wiki/File:Sto._Nino_Basilica_de_Cebu_from_the_Pligrim%27s_Center.jpg)

Catholics attend Mass at [Basilica del Santo Niño](/wiki/Basilica_del_Santo_Ni%C3%B1o "Basilica del Santo Niño") during the annual [Sinulog](/wiki/Sinulog "Sinulog") festival in [Cebu](/wiki/Cebu "Cebu").

Although the Philippines is a [secular state](/wiki/Secularism_in_the_Philippines "Secularism in the Philippines") with [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_the_Philippines "Freedom of religion in the Philippines"), an overwhelming majority of Filipinos [consider religion very important](/wiki/Importance_of_religion_by_country "Importance of religion by country")[[538]](#cite_note-546) and [irreligion](/wiki/Irreligion_in_the_Philippines "Irreligion in the Philippines") is very low.[[539]](#cite_note-547)[[540]](#cite_note-548)[[541]](#cite_note-PSAGovPH-2015Census-Religion-549) [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_Philippines "Christianity in the Philippines") is the dominant religion[[542]](#cite_note-550)[[543]](#cite_note-StateGov-ReligiousFreedom-2015-551) followed by about 89 percent of the population.[[544]](#cite_note-552) The country had the [world's third-largest Roman Catholic population](/wiki/Catholic_Church_by_country "Catholic Church by country") as of 2013[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit), and was Asia's [largest Christian nation](/wiki/Christianity_in_Asia "Christianity in Asia").[[545]](#cite_note-553)

Census data from [2020](/wiki/2020_Philippine_census "2020 Philippine census") found that 78.8 percent of the population professed [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_the_Philippines "Catholic Church in the Philippines").[[i]](#cite_note-Catholic-2020Census-554)[[546]](#cite_note-555) Other [Christian denominations](/wiki/List_of_Christian_denominations_in_the_Philippines "List of Christian denominations in the Philippines") include [Iglesia ni Cristo](/wiki/Iglesia_ni_Cristo "Iglesia ni Cristo"), the [Philippine Independent Church](/wiki/Philippine_Independent_Church "Philippine Independent Church"), and [Seventh-day Adventism](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventist_Church "Seventh-day Adventist Church").[[547]](#cite_note-PSAGovPH-2020Census-Religion-556) [Protestants](/wiki/Protestantism_in_the_Philippines "Protestantism in the Philippines") made up about 5% to 7% of the population in 2010.[[548]](#cite_note-557)[[549]](#cite_note-558) The Philippines sends many [Christian missionaries](/wiki/Christian_mission "Christian mission") around the world, and is a training center for foreign priests and nuns.[[550]](#cite_note-559)[[551]](#cite_note-560)

[Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_the_Philippines "Islam in the Philippines") is the country's second-largest religion, with 6.4 percent of the population in the 2020 census.[[547]](#cite_note-PSAGovPH-2020Census-Religion-556) Most Muslims live in Mindanao and nearby islands,[[543]](#cite_note-StateGov-ReligiousFreedom-2015-551) and most adhere to the [Shafi'i school](/wiki/Shafi%27i_school "Shafi'i school") of [Sunni Islam](/wiki/Sunni_Islam "Sunni Islam").[[552]](#cite_note-561)

About 0.2 percent of the population [follow indigenous religions](/wiki/Indigenous_Philippine_folk_religions "Indigenous Philippine folk religions"),[[547]](#cite_note-PSAGovPH-2020Census-Religion-556) whose practices and folk beliefs are often [syncretized](/wiki/Religious_syncretism "Religious syncretism") with Christianity and Islam.[[231]](#cite_note-Rodell-2002-239): [29–30](https://books.google.com/books?id=y1CVR74_KHQC&pg=PA29)[[553]](#cite_note-562) [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_the_Philippines "Buddhism in the Philippines") is practiced by about 0.04% of the population,[[547]](#cite_note-PSAGovPH-2020Census-Religion-556) primarily by Filipinos of Chinese descent.[[554]](#cite_note-563)

### Health

Main article: [Health in the Philippines](/wiki/Health_in_the_Philippines "Health in the Philippines")

[![A steadily-rising graph until the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Life_expectancy_in_the_Philippines.svg/250px-Life_expectancy_in_the_Philippines.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Life_expectancy_in_the_Philippines.svg)

Life expectancy in the Philippines, 1938–2021

[Health care in the Philippines](/wiki/Health_care_in_the_Philippines "Health care in the Philippines") is provided by the national and local governments, although private payments account for most healthcare spending.[[484]](#cite_note-DOH-2018-492): 25–27[[555]](#cite_note-564) Per-capita health expenditure in 2024 was [₱](/wiki/Philippine_peso "Philippine peso")12,751 and health expenditures were 5.9 percent of the country's GDP.[[556]](#cite_note-565) The 2023 budget allocation for healthcare was ₱334.9 billion.[[557]](#cite_note-OneNews-MarcosSigns2023Budget-566) The 2019 enactment of the [Universal Health Care Act](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") by President Duterte facilitated the automatic enrollment of all Filipinos in the [national health insurance program](/wiki/Philippine_Health_Insurance_Corporation "Philippine Health Insurance Corporation").[[558]](#cite_note-567)[[559]](#cite_note-568) Since 2018, [Malasakit Centers](/wiki/Malasakit_Center "Malasakit Center") (one-stop shops) have been set up in several government-operated hospitals to provide medical and financial assistance to indigent patients.[[560]](#cite_note-569)

Average life expectancy in the Philippines as of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit) is 70.48 years (66.97 years for males, and 74.15 years for females).[[10]](#cite_note-CIAWorldFactBook-15) Access to medicine has improved due to increasing Filipino acceptance of [generic drugs](/wiki/Generic_drug "Generic drug").[[484]](#cite_note-DOH-2018-492): 58 The country's leading causes of death in 2021 were [ischaemic heart diseases](/wiki/Ischaemic_heart_diseases "Ischaemic heart diseases"), [cerebrovascular diseases](/wiki/Cerebrovascular_disease "Cerebrovascular disease"), [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19 "COVID-19"), [neoplasms](/wiki/Neoplasm "Neoplasm"), and [diabetes](/wiki/Diabetes "Diabetes").[[561]](#cite_note-570) [Communicable diseases](/wiki/Communicable_disease "Communicable disease") are correlated with natural disasters, primarily floods.[[562]](#cite_note-571) One million Filipinos have active [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"), the fourth highest global prevalence rate.[[563]](#cite_note-572)

The Philippines has 1,387 [hospitals](/wiki/List_of_hospitals_in_the_Philippines "List of hospitals in the Philippines"), 33 percent of which are government-run; 23,281 barangay health stations, 2,592 rural health units, 2,411 [birthing homes](/wiki/Birthing_center "Birthing center"), and 659 infirmaries provide primary care throughout the country.[[564]](#cite_note-RVONL-HealthCare-2021-573) Since 1967, the Philippines had become the largest global supplier of nurses;[[565]](#cite_note-574) seventy percent of [nursing graduates](/wiki/Nursing_in_the_Philippines "Nursing in the Philippines") go overseas to work, causing problems in retaining skilled practitioners.[[566]](#cite_note-575)

### Education

Main article: [Education in the Philippines](/wiki/Education_in_the_Philippines "Education in the Philippines")

Further information: [Higher education in the Philippines](/wiki/Higher_education_in_the_Philippines "Higher education in the Philippines")

[![Front of a very old building](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Main_Bulding_of_the_University_of_Santo_Tomas.jpg/250px-Main_Bulding_of_the_University_of_Santo_Tomas.jpg)](/wiki/File:Main_Bulding_of_the_University_of_Santo_Tomas.jpg)

Founded in 1611, the [University of Santo Tomas](/wiki/University_of_Santo_Tomas "University of Santo Tomas") is Asia's oldest extant university.[[567]](#cite_note-576)

Primary and secondary schooling in the Philippines consists of six years of elementary period, four years of junior high school, and two years of senior high school.[[568]](#cite_note-577) Public education, provided by the government, is free at the elementary and secondary levels and at [most public higher-education institutions](/wiki/Universal_Access_to_Quality_Tertiary_Education_Act "Universal Access to Quality Tertiary Education Act").[[569]](#cite_note-OECD-2017May-578)[[570]](#cite_note-579) [Science high schools](/wiki/Philippine_Science_High_School_System "Philippine Science High School System") for talented students were established in 1963.[[571]](#cite_note-580) The government provides technical-vocational training and development through the [Technical Education and Skills Development Authority](/wiki/Technical_Education_and_Skills_Development_Authority "Technical Education and Skills Development Authority").[[572]](#cite_note-581) In 2004, the government began offering [alternative education](/wiki/Alternative_Learning_System_(Philippines) "Alternative Learning System (Philippines)") to out-of-school children, youth, and adults to improve literacy;[[573]](#cite_note-582)[[574]](#cite_note-583) [madaris](/wiki/Madrasa "Madrasa") were mainstreamed in 16 regions that year, primarily in Mindanao Muslim areas under the [Department of Education](/wiki/Department_of_Education_(Philippines) "Department of Education (Philippines)").[[575]](#cite_note-584) [Catholic schools](/wiki/List_of_Catholic_universities_and_colleges_in_the_Philippines "List of Catholic universities and colleges in the Philippines"), which number more than 1,500,[[576]](#cite_note-585) and higher education institutions are an integral part of the educational system.[[577]](#cite_note-586)

The Philippines has [1,975 higher education institutions](/wiki/List_of_colleges_and_universities_in_the_Philippines "List of colleges and universities in the Philippines") as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit), of which 246 are public and 1,729 are private.[[578]](#cite_note-587) Public universities are non-sectarian, and are primarily classified as [state-administered](/wiki/List_of_state_schools,_colleges_and_universities_in_the_Philippines "List of state schools, colleges and universities in the Philippines") or [local government-funded](/wiki/Local_colleges_and_universities_(Philippines) "Local colleges and universities (Philippines)").[[579]](#cite_note-588)[[580]](#cite_note-589) The [national university](/wiki/National_university "National university") is the eight-school [University of the Philippines](/wiki/University_of_the_Philippines "University of the Philippines") (UP) system.[[581]](#cite_note-590) The country's top-ranked universities are the [University of the Philippines Diliman](/wiki/University_of_the_Philippines_Diliman "University of the Philippines Diliman"), [Ateneo de Manila University](/wiki/Ateneo_de_Manila_University "Ateneo de Manila University"), [De La Salle University](/wiki/De_La_Salle_University "De La Salle University"), and [University of Santo Tomas](/wiki/University_of_Santo_Tomas "University of Santo Tomas").[[582]](#cite_note-591)[[583]](#cite_note-592)[[584]](#cite_note-593)

In 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit), the Philippines had a basic [literacy](/wiki/Literacy "Literacy") rate of 93.8 percent of those five years old or older,[[585]](#cite_note-594) and a [functional literacy](/wiki/Functional_illiteracy "Functional illiteracy") rate of 91.6 percent of those aged 10 to 64.[[586]](#cite_note-595) Education, a significant proportion of the national budget, was allocated ₱900.9 billion from the ₱5.268 trillion 2023 budget.[[557]](#cite_note-OneNews-MarcosSigns2023Budget-566) As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Philippines&action=edit), the country has 1,640 [public libraries](/wiki/Public_libraries "Public libraries") affiliated with the [National Library of the Philippines](/wiki/National_Library_of_the_Philippines "National Library of the Philippines").[[587]](#cite_note-596)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of the Philippines](/wiki/Culture_of_the_Philippines "Culture of the Philippines")

[![A terraced hillside, seen from above](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/50/Banaue_Philippines_Banaue-Rice-Terraces-01.jpg/250px-Banaue_Philippines_Banaue-Rice-Terraces-01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Banaue_Philippines_Banaue-Rice-Terraces-01.jpg)

The [Banaue Rice Terraces](/wiki/Banaue_Rice_Terraces "Banaue Rice Terraces"), carved by ancestors of the [Ifugao people](/wiki/Ifugao_people "Ifugao people")

The Philippines has significant cultural diversity, reinforced by the country's fragmented geography.[[38]](#cite_note-Junker-1999-45): [61](https://books.google.com/books?id=r50BEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA61)[[588]](#cite_note-Bankoff-2017-597) Spanish and American cultures profoundly influenced Filipino culture as a result of long colonization.[[589]](#cite_note-Edelstein-2011-598)[[288]](#cite_note-Banlaoi-2009-296) The cultures of [Lumads](/wiki/Lumad "Lumad") of Mindanao and the Sulu Archipelago developed distinctly, since they had limited Spanish influence and more influence from nearby Islamic regions.[[51]](#cite_note-Wernstedt-1967-58): [503](https://books.google.com/books?id=6Pn0Pfh1Cl0C&pg=PA503) Indigenous groups such as the [Igorots](/wiki/Igorot "Igorot") have preserved their precolonial customs and traditions by [resisting the Spanish](/wiki/Igorot_resistance_to_Spanish_colonization "Igorot resistance to Spanish colonization").[[590]](#cite_note-599)[[591]](#cite_note-600) A [national identity](/wiki/Filipino_nationalism "Filipino nationalism") [emerged](/wiki/National_revival "National revival") during the 19th century, however, with [shared national symbols](/wiki/National_symbols_of_the_Philippines "National symbols of the Philippines") and cultural and historical [touchstones](/wiki/Touchstone_(metaphor) "Touchstone (metaphor)").[[588]](#cite_note-Bankoff-2017-597)

[Hispanic legacies](/wiki/Spanish_influence_on_Filipino_culture "Spanish influence on Filipino culture") includes the dominance of Catholicism,[[59]](#cite_note-Guillermo-2012-66): [5](https://books.google.com/books?id=wmgX9M_yETIC&pg=PA5)[[589]](#cite_note-Edelstein-2011-598) a [creole language](/wiki/Chavacano "Chavacano"),[[592]](#cite_note-601) numerous [loanwords](/wiki/List_of_loanwords_in_the_Tagalog_language#Spanish "List of loanwords in the Tagalog language"),[[533]](#cite_note-:2-541) the endangered [Philippine Spanish](/wiki/Philippine_Spanish "Philippine Spanish"),[[593]](#cite_note-602) arts,[[594]](#cite_note-:3-603) architecture,[[595]](#cite_note-:4-604) [literature](/wiki/Philippine_literature_in_Spanish "Philippine literature in Spanish"),[[596]](#cite_note-605) drama,[[597]](#cite_note-606) food,[[598]](#cite_note-influ1-607) music,[[599]](#cite_note-608) dance,[[600]](#cite_note-609) fashion[[601]](#cite_note-Coo_2014-610) and the prevalence of [Spanish names and surnames](/wiki/Cat%C3%A1logo_alfab%C3%A9tico_de_apellidos "Catálogo alfabético de apellidos"), which resulted from an 1849 edict ordering the systematic distribution of family names and the implementation of [Spanish naming customs](/wiki/Spanish_naming_customs "Spanish naming customs");[[210]](#cite_note-Boquet-2017-218): [75](https://books.google.com/books?id=90C4DgAAQBAJ&pg=PA75)[[58]](#cite_note-Woods-2006-65): [237](https://books.google.com/books?id=2Z-n_kDTxf0C&pg=PT262) the [names of many locations](/wiki/List_of_Philippine_place_names_of_Spanish_origin "List of Philippine place names of Spanish origin") also have Spanish origins.[[602]](#cite_note-611) American influence on modern Filipino culture[[288]](#cite_note-Banlaoi-2009-296) is evident in the use of English,[[603]](#cite_note-Nadal-2011-612): [12](https://books.google.com/books?id=zN7-s84jAkoC&pg=PA12) and Filipino consumption of [fast food](/wiki/Fast_food "Fast food") and American films and music.[[589]](#cite_note-Edelstein-2011-598)

[Public holidays in the Philippines](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_the_Philippines "Public holidays in the Philippines") are classified as regular or special.[[604]](#cite_note-613) [Festivals](/wiki/List_of_festivals_in_the_Philippines "List of festivals in the Philippines") are primarily religious, and most towns and villages have such a festival (usually to honor a [patron saint](/wiki/Patron_saint "Patron saint")).[[605]](#cite_note-614)[[606]](#cite_note-Wendt-1998-615) Better-known festivals include [Ati-Atihan](/wiki/Ati-Atihan_festival "Ati-Atihan festival"),[[607]](#cite_note-616) [Dinagyang](/wiki/Dinagyang "Dinagyang"),[[608]](#cite_note-617) [Moriones](/wiki/Moriones_Festival "Moriones Festival"),[[609]](#cite_note-618) [Sinulog](/wiki/Sinulog "Sinulog"),[[610]](#cite_note-619) and [Flores de Mayo](/wiki/Flores_de_Mayo "Flores de Mayo")—a month-long devotion to the [Virgin Mary](/wiki/Virgin_Mary "Virgin Mary") held in May.[[611]](#cite_note-620) The country's [Christmas season](/wiki/Christmas_in_the_Philippines "Christmas in the Philippines") begins as early as September 1,[[612]](#cite_note-Rood-2019-621): [149](https://books.google.com/books?id=JmCpDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA149) and [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week_in_the_Philippines "Holy Week in the Philippines") is a solemn religious observance for its Christian population.[[613]](#cite_note-622)[[612]](#cite_note-Rood-2019-621): [149](https://books.google.com/books?id=JmCpDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA149)

### Values

Main article: [Filipino values](/wiki/Filipino_values "Filipino values")

Further information: [Filipino psychology](/wiki/Filipino_psychology "Filipino psychology")

[![Colored outdoor statue of a child pressing their forehead on the hand of a seated elder](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Core_Value.JPG/250px-Core_Value.JPG)](/wiki/File:Core_Value.JPG)

Statue in [Iriga](/wiki/Iriga "Iriga") commemorating *[mano po](/wiki/Mano_(gesture) "Mano (gesture)")*

Filipino [values](/wiki/Value_(ethics_and_social_sciences) "Value (ethics and social sciences)") are rooted primarily in personal alliances based in [kinship](/wiki/Philippine_kinship "Philippine kinship"), obligation, friendship, religion (particularly Christianity), and commerce.[[82]](#cite_note-Dolan-1991-89): [41](https://web.archive.org/web/20060927161138/https://countrystudies.us/philippines/41.htm) They center around social harmony through *[pakikisama](/wiki/Pakikisama "Pakikisama")*,[[614]](#cite_note-Qiu-2004-623): [74](https://books.google.com/books?id=puYpTx1gE40C&pg=PA74) motivated primarily by the desire for acceptance by a group.[[615]](#cite_note-624)[[616]](#cite_note-Warner-2014-625)[[603]](#cite_note-Nadal-2011-612): [47](https://books.google.com/books?id=zN7-s84jAkoC&pg=PA47) Reciprocity through *[utang na loob](/wiki/Utang_na_loob "Utang na loob")* (a debt of gratitude) is a significant Filipino cultural trait, and an internalized debt can never be fully repaid.[[614]](#cite_note-Qiu-2004-623): [76](https://books.google.com/books?id=puYpTx1gE40C&pg=PA76)[[617]](#cite_note-626) The main sanction for divergence from these values are the concepts of *hiya* (shame)[[618]](#cite_note-627) and loss of *amor propio* ([self-esteem](/wiki/Self-esteem "Self-esteem")).[[616]](#cite_note-Warner-2014-625)

The family is central to Philippine society; norms such as loyalty, maintaining close relationships and care for elderly parents are ingrained in Philippine society.[[619]](#cite_note-628)[[620]](#cite_note-629) Respect for authority and the elderly is valued, and is shown with gestures such as *mano* and the [honorifics](/wiki/Filipino_styles_and_honorifics "Filipino styles and honorifics") *po* and *opo* and *kuya* (older brother) or *ate* (older sister).[[621]](#cite_note-630)[[622]](#cite_note-631) Other Filipino values are optimism about the future, pessimism about the present, concern about other people, friendship and friendliness, [hospitality](/wiki/Hospitality "Hospitality"), religiosity, respect for oneself and others (particularly [women](/wiki/Women_in_the_Philippines "Women in the Philippines")), and integrity.[[623]](#cite_note-632)

### Art and architecture

Main articles: [Arts in the Philippines](/wiki/Arts_in_the_Philippines "Arts in the Philippines") and [Architecture of the Philippines](/wiki/Architecture_of_the_Philippines "Architecture of the Philippines")

Philippine art combines indigenous folk art, east-Asian and [Classical traditions](/wiki/Classical_art "Classical art").[[624]](#cite_note-633)[[625]](#cite_note-McFerson-2002-634)[[594]](#cite_note-:3-603) During the Spanish colonial period, art was used to spread Catholicism mainly through paintings and sculptures. The first recorded sculptor in the Philippines is Juan de los Santos (1590 –1660) known for making retablos.[[626]](#cite_note-:1-635) In 1821, [Damian Domingo](/wiki/Dami%C3%A1n_Domingo "Damián Domingo"), the father of Filipino paintings, opened the [*Academia de Dibujo*](/wiki/Academia_de_Dibujo_y_Pintura "Academia de Dibujo y Pintura") art school in Binondo.[[626]](#cite_note-:1-635)[[627]](#cite_note-636) Other artists during Spanish colonial rule included painters such as Josef Luciano Dans, [Jose Honorato Lozano](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Honorato_Lozano "José Honorato Lozano"), Mariano Asuncion[[628]](#cite_note-637) and sculptors such as [Isabelo Tampinco](/wiki/Isabelo_Tampinco "Isabelo Tampinco") and Crispulo Hocson.[[626]](#cite_note-:1-635) Artist whose works drew attention to the Philippines are [Juan Luna](/wiki/Juan_Luna "Juan Luna") and [Félix Resurrección Hidalgo](/wiki/F%C3%A9lix_Resurrecci%C3%B3n_Hidalgo "Félix Resurrección Hidalgo").[[629]](#cite_note-638) [Fernando Amorsolo](/wiki/Fernando_Amorsolo "Fernando Amorsolo") dominated Philippine paintings during the American colonial period, popular for his [pastoral](/wiki/Pastoral "Pastoral") scenes of Philippine countryside.[[630]](#cite_note-NCCA-639)[[631]](#cite_note-640) [Victorio Edades](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") known as the father of Modern Philippine Art, popularized [Modernism](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") in the Philippines in 1920s and 1930s.[[632]](#cite_note-641)

Traditional Philippine architecture has two main models: the indigenous *[bahay kubo](/wiki/Bahay_kubo "Bahay kubo")* and the *[bahay na bato](/wiki/Bahay_na_bato "Bahay na bato")*, which developed under Spanish rule.[[210]](#cite_note-Boquet-2017-218): [438–444](https://books.google.com/books?id=90C4DgAAQBAJ&pg=PA438) Some regions, such as [Batanes](/wiki/Batanes "Batanes"), differ slightly due to climate; limestone was used as a building material, and houses were built to withstand typhoons.[[633]](#cite_note-642)[[634]](#cite_note-643)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/ZAMBOANGA_CITY_Asia%27s_Latin_City_City_Hall_and_Plaza_Rizal_%28Ayunamiento_y_Plaza_Rizal%29.jpg/250px-ZAMBOANGA_CITY_Asia%27s_Latin_City_City_Hall_and_Plaza_Rizal_%28Ayunamiento_y_Plaza_Rizal%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:ZAMBOANGA_CITY_Asia%27s_Latin_City_City_Hall_and_Plaza_Rizal_(Ayunamiento_y_Plaza_Rizal).jpg)

[Zamboanga City](/wiki/Zamboanga_City "Zamboanga City") [Hall](/wiki/Zamboanga_City_Hall "Zamboanga City Hall")[[635]](#cite_note-bm-644)

[Spanish architecture](/wiki/Spanish_architecture "Spanish architecture") left an imprint in town designs around a [central square](/wiki/Poblacion "Poblacion") or *plaza mayor*, but many of its buildings were damaged or destroyed during World War II.[[595]](#cite_note-:4-604)[[49]](#cite_note-Ring-1996-56) [Several](/wiki/List_of_Baroque_churches_in_the_Philippines "List of Baroque churches in the Philippines") Philippine [churches](/wiki/List_of_Catholic_churches_in_the_Philippines "List of Catholic churches in the Philippines") adapted [baroque architecture](/wiki/Baroque_architecture "Baroque architecture") to withstand earthquakes, leading to the development of [Earthquake Baroque](/wiki/Earthquake_Baroque "Earthquake Baroque");[[636]](#cite_note-645)[[637]](#cite_note-Girard-2021-646) [four baroque churches](/wiki/Baroque_Churches_of_the_Philippines "Baroque Churches of the Philippines") have been listed as a collective UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").[[638]](#cite_note-UNESCO-BaroqueChurches-647) [Spanish colonial fortifications](/wiki/Spanish_colonial_fortifications_in_the_Philippines "Spanish colonial fortifications in the Philippines") (*fuerzas*) in several parts of the Philippines were primarily designed by missionary architects and built by Filipino stonemasons.[[639]](#cite_note-648) [Vigan](/wiki/Vigan "Vigan"), in [Ilocos Sur](/wiki/Ilocos_Sur "Ilocos Sur"), is known for its Hispanic-style houses and buildings.[[640]](#cite_note-649)

American rule introduced new architectural styles in the construction of government buildings and [Art Deco](/wiki/Art_Deco "Art Deco") theaters.[[641]](#cite_note-NCCAGovPH-Architecture-650) During the American period, construction of [Gabaldon school buildings](/wiki/Gabaldon_school_buildings "Gabaldon school buildings") began,[[642]](#cite_note-651) and some city planning using architectural designs and master plans by [Daniel Burnham](/wiki/Daniel_Burnham "Daniel Burnham") was done in portions of [Manila](/wiki/Burnham_Plan_of_Manila "Burnham Plan of Manila") and [Baguio](/wiki/Baguio "Baguio").[[643]](#cite_note-652)[[644]](#cite_note-653) Part of the Burnham plan was the construction of government buildings reminiscent of [Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_architecture "Ancient Greek architecture") or [Neoclassical architecture](/wiki/Neoclassical_architecture "Neoclassical architecture").[[641]](#cite_note-NCCAGovPH-Architecture-650)[[637]](#cite_note-Girard-2021-646) Buildings from the Spanish and American periods can be seen in [Iloilo](/wiki/Iloilo "Iloilo"), especially in [Calle Real](/wiki/Calle_Real,_Iloilo "Calle Real, Iloilo").[[645]](#cite_note-654)

### Music and dance

Main articles: [Music of the Philippines](/wiki/Music_of_the_Philippines "Music of the Philippines") and [Dance in the Philippines](/wiki/Dance_in_the_Philippines "Dance in the Philippines")

[![Female dancers in colorful dresses](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Tinikling.jpg/250px-Tinikling.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tinikling.jpg)

[Tinikling](/wiki/Tinikling "Tinikling"), a dance depicting the swift leg movements of a *tikling* bird eluding a farmer's traps[[646]](#cite_note-655)

There are two types of Philippine [folk dance](/wiki/Folk_dance "Folk dance"), stemming from traditional indigenous influences and Spanish influence.[[231]](#cite_note-Rodell-2002-239): [173](https://books.google.com/books?id=y1CVR74_KHQC&pg=PA173) Although native dances had become less popular,[[647]](#cite_note-Villaruz-2006-656): [77](https://books.google.com/books?id=qLQOd-PFxe4C&pg=PA77) folk dancing began to revive during the 1920s.[[647]](#cite_note-Villaruz-2006-656): [82](https://books.google.com/books?id=qLQOd-PFxe4C&pg=PA82) The [Cariñosa](/wiki/Cari%C3%B1osa "Cariñosa"), a Hispanic Filipino dance, is unofficially considered the country's national dance.[[648]](#cite_note-657) Popular indigenous dances include the [Tinikling](/wiki/Tinikling "Tinikling") and [Singkil](/wiki/Singkil_(Maranao_dance) "Singkil (Maranao dance)"), which include the rhythmic clapping of bamboo poles.[[649]](#cite_note-658)[[650]](#cite_note-659) Present-day dances vary from delicate [ballet](/wiki/Ballet "Ballet")[[651]](#cite_note-660) to street-oriented [breakdancing](/wiki/Breakdancing "Breakdancing").[[652]](#cite_note-661)[[653]](#cite_note-662)

[Rondalya](/wiki/Rondalla "Rondalla") music, with traditional [mandolin](/wiki/Mandolin "Mandolin")-type [instruments](/wiki/List_of_Philippine_musical_instruments "List of Philippine musical instruments"), was popular during the Spanish era.[[161]](#cite_note-Thompson-Batalla-2018-169): [327](https://books.google.com/books?id=DmkPEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA327)[[654]](#cite_note-KCET-MasterRondalla-663) Spanish-influenced musicians are primarily [bandurria](/wiki/Bandurria "Bandurria")-based bands with 14-string guitars.[[655]](#cite_note-664)[[654]](#cite_note-KCET-MasterRondalla-663) [Kundiman](/wiki/Kundiman "Kundiman") developed during the 1920s and 1930s.[[656]](#cite_note-Ellingham-1999-665) The American colonial period exposed many Filipinos to [U.S. culture](/wiki/Culture_of_the_United_States "Culture of the United States") and [popular music](/wiki/Music_of_the_United_States "Music of the United States").[[656]](#cite_note-Ellingham-1999-665) [Rock music](/wiki/Rock_music "Rock music") was introduced to Filipinos during the 1960s and developed into Filipino rock (or [Pinoy rock](/wiki/Pinoy_rock "Pinoy rock")), a term encompassing [pop rock](/wiki/Pop_rock "Pop rock"), [alternative rock](/wiki/Alternative_rock "Alternative rock"), [heavy metal](/wiki/Heavy_metal_music "Heavy metal music"), [punk](/wiki/Punk_rock "Punk rock"), [new wave](/wiki/New_wave_music "New wave music"), [ska](/wiki/Ska "Ska"), and [reggae](/wiki/Reggae "Reggae"). Martial law in the 1970s produced [Filipino folk rock](/wiki/Philippine_folk_music "Philippine folk music") bands and artists who [were at the forefront](/wiki/Protest_music_against_the_Marcos_dictatorship "Protest music against the Marcos dictatorship") of political demonstrations.[[657]](#cite_note-Murray-2016-666): [38–41](https://books.google.com/books?id=ot2uDAAAQBAJ&pg=PA38) The decade also saw the birth of the [Manila sound](/wiki/Manila_sound "Manila sound") and [Original Pilipino Music](/wiki/Original_Pilipino_Music "Original Pilipino Music") (OPM).[[658]](#cite_note-Shunwei-2022-667)[[58]](#cite_note-Woods-2006-65): [171](https://books.google.com/books?id=2Z-n_kDTxf0C&pg=PT196) [Filipino hip-hop](/wiki/Filipino_hip-hop "Filipino hip-hop"), which originated in 1979, entered the mainstream in 1990.[[659]](#cite_note-668)[[657]](#cite_note-Murray-2016-666): [38–41](https://books.google.com/books?id=ot2uDAAAQBAJ&pg=PA38) [Karaoke](/wiki/Karaoke "Karaoke") is also popular.[[660]](#cite_note-669) From 2010 to 2020, [Pinoy pop](/wiki/Pinoy_pop "Pinoy pop") (P-pop) was influenced by [K-pop](/wiki/K-pop "K-pop") and [J-pop](/wiki/J-pop "J-pop").[[661]](#cite_note-670)

Locally produced theatrical drama became established during the late 1870s. Spanish influence around that time introduced *[zarzuela](/wiki/Zarzuela "Zarzuela")* plays (with music)[[662]](#cite_note-Liu-2016-671) and *[comedia](/wiki/Comedia_(play) "Comedia (play)")*s, with dance. The plays became popular throughout the country,[[647]](#cite_note-Villaruz-2006-656): [69–70](https://books.google.com/books?id=qLQOd-PFxe4C&pg=PA69) and were written in a number of local languages.[[662]](#cite_note-Liu-2016-671) American influence introduced [vaudeville](/wiki/Vaudeville "Vaudeville") and ballet.[[647]](#cite_note-Villaruz-2006-656): [69–70](https://books.google.com/books?id=qLQOd-PFxe4C&pg=PA69) [Realistic](/wiki/Realism_(theatre) "Realism (theatre)") theater became dominant during the 20th century, with plays focusing on contemporary political and social issues.[[662]](#cite_note-Liu-2016-671)

### Literature

Main article: [Philippine literature](/wiki/Philippine_literature "Philippine literature")

[![photograph of José Rizal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Jose_Rizal_full.jpg/250px-Jose_Rizal_full.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jose_Rizal_full.jpg)

José Rizal's writings inspired the [Philippine Revolution](/wiki/Philippine_Revolution "Philippine Revolution").

Philippine literature consists of [works](/wiki/Filipiniana "Filipiniana") usually written in Filipino, [Spanish](/wiki/Philippine_literature_in_Spanish "Philippine literature in Spanish"), or [English](/wiki/Philippine_literature_in_English "Philippine literature in English"). Some of the earliest well-known works were created from the 17th to the 19th centuries.[[663]](#cite_note-672) They include *[Ibong Adarna](/wiki/Ibong_Adarna "Ibong Adarna")*, an epic about an eponymous magical bird,[[664]](#cite_note-673) and *[Florante at Laura](/wiki/Florante_at_Laura "Florante at Laura")* by Tagalog author [Francisco Balagtas](/wiki/Francisco_Balagtas "Francisco Balagtas").[[665]](#cite_note-674)[[666]](#cite_note-675) [José Rizal](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Rizal "José Rizal") wrote the novels *[Noli Me Tángere](/wiki/Noli_Me_T%C3%A1ngere_(novel) "Noli Me Tángere (novel)")* and *[El filibusterismo](/wiki/El_filibusterismo "El filibusterismo")*,[[667]](#cite_note-676) both of which depict the injustices of Spanish colonial rule.[[668]](#cite_note-677)

[Folk literature](/wiki/Philippine_folk_literature "Philippine folk literature") was relatively unaffected by colonial influence until the 19th century due to Spanish indifference. Most printed literary works during Spanish colonial rule were religious in nature, although [Filipino elites](/wiki/Ilustrado "Ilustrado") who later learned Spanish wrote [nationalistic](/wiki/Filipino_nationalism "Filipino nationalism") literature.[[231]](#cite_note-Rodell-2002-239): [59–62](https://books.google.com/books?id=y1CVR74_KHQC&pg=PA59) The American arrival [began](/wiki/Education_in_the_Philippines_during_American_rule "Education in the Philippines during American rule") Filipino literary use of English[[231]](#cite_note-Rodell-2002-239): [65–66](https://books.google.com/books?id=y1CVR74_KHQC&pg=PA65) and influenced the development of the [Philippine comics](/wiki/Philippine_comics "Philippine comics") industry that flourished from the 1920s through the 1970s.[[669]](#cite_note-678)[[670]](#cite_note-679) In the late 1960s, during the presidency of Ferdinand Marcos, Philippine literature was [influenced by political activism](/wiki/Student_activism_in_the_Philippines_(1965%E2%80%931972) "Student activism in the Philippines (1965–1972)"); many poets began using Tagalog, in keeping with the country's oral traditions.[[231]](#cite_note-Rodell-2002-239): [69–71](https://books.google.com/books?id=y1CVR74_KHQC&pg=PA69)

[Philippine mythology](/wiki/Philippine_mythology "Philippine mythology") has been handed down primarily through oral tradition;[[671]](#cite_note-680) popular [figures](/wiki/List_of_Philippine_mythological_figures "List of Philippine mythological figures") are [Maria Makiling](/wiki/Maria_Makiling "Maria Makiling"),[[672]](#cite_note-681) [Lam-ang](/wiki/Biag_ni_Lam-Ang "Biag ni Lam-Ang"),[[673]](#cite_note-682) and the [Sarimanok](/wiki/Sarimanok "Sarimanok").[[231]](#cite_note-Rodell-2002-239): [61](https://books.google.com/books?id=y1CVR74_KHQC&pg=PA61)[[674]](#cite_note-683) The country has a number of [folk epics](/wiki/Philippine_epic_poetry "Philippine epic poetry").[[675]](#cite_note-684) Wealthy families could preserve transcriptions of the epics as family heirlooms, particularly in Mindanao; the [Maranao-language](/wiki/Maranao_language "Maranao language") *[Darangen](/wiki/Darangen "Darangen")* is an example.[[676]](#cite_note-685)

### Media

Main article: [Mass media in the Philippines](/wiki/Mass_media_in_the_Philippines "Mass media in the Philippines")

Further information: [Cinema of the Philippines](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_Philippines "Cinema of the Philippines")

[![TV network logo, a multicolored triangle](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/People%27s_Television_Network_Logo_%282017-present%29.jpg/250px-People%27s_Television_Network_Logo_%282017-present%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:People%27s_Television_Network_Logo_(2017-present).jpg)

People's Television Network logo

Philippine media primarily uses Filipino and English, although [broadcasting](/wiki/Broadcasting "Broadcasting") has shifted to Filipino.[[528]](#cite_note-Brown-Ganguly-2003-536) [Television shows](/wiki/List_of_Philippine_television_shows "List of Philippine television shows"), commercials, and [films](/wiki/Lists_of_Philippine_films "Lists of Philippine films") are regulated by the [Movie and Television Review and Classification Board](/wiki/Movie_and_Television_Review_and_Classification_Board "Movie and Television Review and Classification Board").[[677]](#cite_note-686)[[678]](#cite_note-687) Most Filipinos obtain news and information from television, the Internet,[[679]](#cite_note-688) and [social media](/wiki/Social_media_use_in_the_Philippines "Social media use in the Philippines").[[680]](#cite_note-CNNPH-SWSFacebook-689) The country's flagship state-owned broadcast-television network is the [People's Television Network](/wiki/People%27s_Television_Network "People's Television Network") (PTV).[[681]](#cite_note-690) [ABS-CBN](/wiki/ABS-CBN "ABS-CBN") and [GMA](/wiki/GMA_Network "GMA Network"), both [free-to-air](/wiki/Free-to-air "Free-to-air"), were the dominant TV networks;[[682]](#cite_note-BBC-Philippines-Profile-691) before the May 2020 [Philippine government denial of ABS-CBN's franchise renewal](/wiki/ABS-CBN_franchise_renewal_controversy "ABS-CBN franchise renewal controversy"), it was the country's largest network.[[683]](#cite_note-692) [Philippine television dramas](/wiki/Philippine_television_drama "Philippine television drama"), known as *teleserye*s and mainly produced by [ABS-CBN](/wiki/List_of_ABS-CBN_original_drama_series "List of ABS-CBN original drama series") and [GMA](/wiki/List_of_GMA_Network_original_drama_series "List of GMA Network original drama series"), are also seen in several other countries.[[684]](#cite_note-693)[[685]](#cite_note-694)

Local film-making [began in 1919](/wiki/List_of_Philippine_films_before_1940 "List of Philippine films before 1940") with the release of the first Filipino-produced [feature film](/wiki/Feature_film "Feature film"): *[Dalagang Bukid](/wiki/Dalagang_Bukid "Dalagang Bukid")* (*A Girl from the Country*), directed by [Jose Nepomuceno](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Nepomuceno "José Nepomuceno").[[118]](#cite_note-Armes-1987-126)[[119]](#cite_note-Tofighian-2006-127): 8 [Production companies](/wiki/List_of_Philippine_film_studios "List of Philippine film studios") remained small during the [silent film](/wiki/Silent_film "Silent film") era, but sound films and larger productions emerged in 1933. The postwar 1940s to the early 1960s are considered a high point for Philippine cinema. The 1962–1971 decade saw a decline in quality films, although the commercial film industry expanded until the 1980s.[[118]](#cite_note-Armes-1987-126) Critically acclaimed Philippine films include *[Himala](/wiki/Himala "Himala")* (*Miracle*) and *[Oro, Plata, Mata](/wiki/Oro,_Plata,_Mata "Oro, Plata, Mata")* (*Gold, Silver, Death*), both released in 1982.[[686]](#cite_note-695)[[687]](#cite_note-696) Since the turn of the 21st century, the country's film industry has struggled to compete with larger-budget foreign films[[688]](#cite_note-697) (particularly [Hollywood films](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States")).[[689]](#cite_note-698)[[690]](#cite_note-699) [Art films](/wiki/Art_film "Art film") have thrived, however, and several [indie films](/wiki/Philippine_New_Wave "Philippine New Wave") have been successful domestically and abroad.[[691]](#cite_note-Forbes-CinemaGrowingFast-700)[[692]](#cite_note-701)[[693]](#cite_note-702)

The Philippines has a large number of [radio stations](/wiki/List_of_radio_stations_in_the_Philippines "List of radio stations in the Philippines") and [newspapers](/wiki/List_of_newspapers_in_the_Philippines "List of newspapers in the Philippines").[[682]](#cite_note-BBC-Philippines-Profile-691) English [broadsheets](/wiki/Broadsheet "Broadsheet") are popular among executives, professionals and students.[[122]](#cite_note-Thompson-2003-130): [233–251](https://books.google.com/books?id=W1h9oF9rj-MC&pg=PA233) Less-expensive Tagalog [tabloids](/wiki/Tabloid_(newspaper_format) "Tabloid (newspaper format)"), which grew during the 1990s, are popular (particularly in Manila);[[694]](#cite_note-703) however, overall newspaper readership is declining in favor of [online news](/wiki/Social_media_as_a_news_source "Social media as a news source").[[680]](#cite_note-CNNPH-SWSFacebook-689)[[695]](#cite_note-704) The top three newspapers, by nationwide readership and credibility,[[122]](#cite_note-Thompson-2003-130): [233](https://books.google.com/books?id=1VI9AAAAQBAJ&pg=PA233) are the *[Philippine Daily Inquirer](/wiki/Philippine_Daily_Inquirer "Philippine Daily Inquirer")*, *[Manila Bulletin](/wiki/Manila_Bulletin "Manila Bulletin")*, and *[The Philippine Star](/wiki/The_Philippine_Star "The Philippine Star")*.[[696]](#cite_note-705)[[697]](#cite_note-706) Although [freedom of the press](/wiki/Freedom_of_the_press "Freedom of the press") is protected by the constitution,[[698]](#cite_note-707) the country was listed as the seventh-most-dangerous country for journalists in 2022 by the [Committee to Protect Journalists](/wiki/Committee_to_Protect_Journalists "Committee to Protect Journalists") due to 13 unsolved murders of journalists.[[699]](#cite_note-708)

The Philippine population are the world's top Internet users.[[700]](#cite_note-709) In early 2021, 67 percent of Filipinos (73.91 million) had Internet access; the overwhelming majority used [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone").[[701]](#cite_note-710)

### Cuisine

Main article: [Filipino cuisine](/wiki/Filipino_cuisine "Filipino cuisine")

[![Chunky soup in a white bowl](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Fish_sinigang.jpg/250px-Fish_sinigang.jpg)](/wiki/File:Fish_sinigang.jpg)

A bowl of fish *[sinigang](/wiki/Sinigang "Sinigang")*

From its Malayo-Polynesian origins, traditional Philippine cuisine has evolved since the 16th century. It was primarily influenced by Hispanic, [Chinese](/wiki/Filipino_Chinese_cuisine "Filipino Chinese cuisine"), and [American](/wiki/American_cuisine "American cuisine") cuisines, which were adapted to the Filipino palate.[[702]](#cite_note-711)[[703]](#cite_note-712) Filipinos tend to prefer robust flavors,[[704]](#cite_note-713) centered on sweet, salty, and sour combinations.[[705]](#cite_note-Aquino-Porter-2022-714): [88](https://books.google.com/books?id=ldCPEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA88) Regional variations exist throughout the country; rice is the general staple [starch](/wiki/Starch#Food "Starch")[[706]](#cite_note-715) but [cassava](/wiki/Cassava-based_dishes "Cassava-based dishes") is more common in parts of Mindanao.[[707]](#cite_note-716)[[708]](#cite_note-717) [Adobo](/wiki/Philippine_adobo "Philippine adobo") is the unofficial national dish.[[709]](#cite_note-718) Other [popular dishes](/wiki/List_of_Philippine_dishes "List of Philippine dishes") include *[lechón](/wiki/Lech%C3%B3n "Lechón")*, *[kare-kare](/wiki/Kare-kare "Kare-kare")*, *[sinigang](/wiki/Sinigang "Sinigang")*,[[710]](#cite_note-719) *[pancit](/wiki/Pancit "Pancit")*, *[lumpia](/wiki/Lumpia "Lumpia")*, and *[arroz caldo](/wiki/Arroz_caldo "Arroz caldo")*.[[711]](#cite_note-Anderson-2018-720)[[712]](#cite_note-721)[[713]](#cite_note-722) [Traditional desserts](/wiki/List_of_Philippine_desserts "List of Philippine desserts") are *kakanin* ([rice cakes](/wiki/Rice_cake "Rice cake")), which include *[puto](/wiki/Puto_(food) "Puto (food)")*, *[suman](/wiki/Suman_(food) "Suman (food)")*, and *[bibingka](/wiki/Bibingka "Bibingka")*.[[714]](#cite_note-723)[[715]](#cite_note-724) Ingredients such as [calamansi](/wiki/Calamansi "Calamansi"),[[716]](#cite_note-725) *[ube](/wiki/Ube_halaya "Ube halaya")*,[[717]](#cite_note-726) and *[pili](/wiki/Canarium_ovatum "Canarium ovatum")* are used in Filipino desserts.[[718]](#cite_note-727)[[719]](#cite_note-728) The generous use of [condiments](/wiki/Philippine_condiments "Philippine condiments") such as *[patis](/wiki/Patis_(sauce) "Patis (sauce)")*, *[bagoong](/wiki/Bagoong "Bagoong")*, and *[toyo](/wiki/Soy_sauce#Filipino "Soy sauce")* impart a distinctive Philippine flavor.[[711]](#cite_note-Anderson-2018-720)[[705]](#cite_note-Aquino-Porter-2022-714): [73](https://books.google.com/books?id=ldCPEAAAQBAJ&pg=PA73)There are vegan filipino food and they include laing, lumpiang gulay, ensaladang talong, turon, ginataang bilo-bilo, biko, espasol, banana cue, camote cue, taho, suman, carioca, and lumpiang sariwa.

Unlike other East or Southeast Asian countries, most Filipinos do not eat with [chopsticks](/wiki/Chopsticks "Chopsticks"); they use spoons and forks.[[j]](#cite_note-729)[[720]](#cite_note-730) Traditional eating with the hands or fingers[[721]](#cite_note-731) (known as *[kamayan](/wiki/Kamayan "Kamayan")*) had been used in less urbanized areas,[[722]](#cite_note-732): [266–268, 277](https://books.google.com/books?id=y6vTun3i4NQC&pg=PA266) but has been popularized with the introduction of Filipino food to foreigners and city residents.[[723]](#cite_note-733)[[724]](#cite_note-734)

### Sports and recreation

Main articles: [Sports in the Philippines](/wiki/Sports_in_the_Philippines "Sports in the Philippines") and [Traditional games in the Philippines](/wiki/Traditional_games_in_the_Philippines "Traditional games in the Philippines")

[![Team photo, with each blue-uniformed member wearing a gold medal](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/Gilas_Cadets_2015_SEA_Games.jpg/250px-Gilas_Cadets_2015_SEA_Games.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gilas_Cadets_2015_SEA_Games.jpg)

The [Philippines men's national basketball team](/wiki/Philippines_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Philippines men's national basketball team") celebrating their [2015 Southeast Asian Games](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2015_Southeast_Asian_Games "Basketball at the 2015 Southeast Asian Games") championship

[Basketball](/wiki/Basketball_in_the_Philippines "Basketball in the Philippines"), played at the amateur and professional levels, is considered the country's most popular sport.[[725]](#cite_note-735)[[726]](#cite_note-736) Other popular sports include [boxing](/wiki/History_of_boxing_in_the_Philippines "History of boxing in the Philippines") and billiards, boosted by the achievements of [Manny Pacquiao](/wiki/Manny_Pacquiao "Manny Pacquiao") and [Efren Reyes](/wiki/Efren_Reyes "Efren Reyes").[[612]](#cite_note-Rood-2019-621): [142](https://books.google.com/books?id=LmmtDwAAQBAJ&pg=PA142)[[727]](#cite_note-737) The national [martial art](/wiki/Filipino_martial_arts "Filipino martial arts") is [Arnis](/wiki/Arnis "Arnis").[[728]](#cite_note-738) *Sabong* ([cockfighting](/wiki/Cockfight "Cockfight")) is popular entertainment, especially among Filipino men, and was documented by the [Magellan expedition](/wiki/Magellan_expedition "Magellan expedition").[[729]](#cite_note-739) [Video gaming](/wiki/Video_games_in_the_Philippines "Video games in the Philippines") and [esports](/wiki/Esports_in_the_Philippines "Esports in the Philippines") are emerging pastimes,[[730]](#cite_note-740)[[731]](#cite_note-Booc-2019-741) with the popularity of [indigenous games](/wiki/Traditional_games_in_the_Philippines "Traditional games in the Philippines") such as *[patintero](/wiki/Patintero "Patintero")*, *[tumbang preso](/wiki/Tumbang_preso "Tumbang preso")*, *[luksong tinik](/wiki/Luksong_tinik "Luksong tinik")*, and *[piko](/wiki/Piko_(Filipino_game) "Piko (Filipino game)")* declining among young people.[[732]](#cite_note-742)[[731]](#cite_note-Booc-2019-741)

The [men's national football team](/wiki/Philippines_national_football_team "Philippines national football team") has participated in one [Asian Cup](/wiki/2019_AFC_Asian_Cup "2019 AFC Asian Cup").[[733]](#cite_note-743) The [women's national football team](/wiki/Philippines_women%27s_national_football_team "Philippines women's national football team") qualified for the [2023 FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/2023_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "2023 FIFA Women's World Cup"), their first [World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "FIFA Women's World Cup"), in January 2022.[[734]](#cite_note-744) The Philippines has participated in every [Summer Olympic Games](/wiki/Summer_Olympic_Games "Summer Olympic Games") since [1924](/wiki/Philippines_at_the_1924_Summer_Olympics "Philippines at the 1924 Summer Olympics"), except when they supported the [American-led boycott of the 1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics_boycott "1980 Summer Olympics boycott").[[735]](#cite_note-745)[[736]](#cite_note-746) It was the first [tropical nation](/wiki/Tropical_nations_at_the_Winter_Olympics "Tropical nations at the Winter Olympics") to compete at the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games"), [debuting in 1972](/wiki/Philippines_at_the_1972_Winter_Olympics "Philippines at the 1972 Winter Olympics").[[737]](#cite_note-747)[[738]](#cite_note-748) In 2021, the Philippines received its first-ever Olympic gold medal with [weightlifter](/wiki/Olympic_weightlifting "Olympic weightlifting") [Hidilyn Diaz](/wiki/Hidilyn_Diaz "Hidilyn Diaz")'s victory in [Tokyo](/wiki/2020_Summer_Olympics "2020 Summer Olympics").[[739]](#cite_note-749)

10. **[^](#cite_ref-729)** In the Philippines, eating with chopsticks is occasionally used, less commonly used, less frequently used, seldom used, very rarely or rarely used, or not very common, but it's perfectly acceptable, especially in certain settings. Most Filipinos use a spoon and fork as the standard utensils for eating, while chopsticks are usually reserved for Asian cuisines like Chinese, Japanese, or Korean food, particularly in restaurants that serve those dishes. At home, chopsticks aren't used daily, though some families keep them for fun or when enjoying Asian meals. There's no stigma in using chopsticks — it's even seen as interesting or "international". Just remember to use them properly, such as avoiding sticking them upright in rice, which is considered disrespectful in many Asian cultures. However, chopsticks are generally used only when eating Chinese or other Asian cuisine that traditionally requires them.
